title-group.article-title: Applicability and adaptation of cognitive behavior therapy for long COVID neuropsychiatric symptoms: a review with insights from ME/CFS

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/0254bmq54grid.419280.60000 0004 1763 8916National Center for Cognitive Behavior Therapy and Research, National Center of Neurology and Psychiatry, 4-1-1 Ogawa-Higashi, Kodaira, 187-8551 Tokyo Japan
2https://ror.org/022cvpj02grid.412708.80000 0004 1764 7572Department of Neuropsychiatry, The University of Tokyo Hospital, 7-3‐1 Hongo, Bunkyo‐ku, Tokyo, 113‐8655 Japan

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Long COVID presents a spectrum of persistent symptoms that substantially overlap with myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS), including debilitating fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cognitive dysfunction, and neuropsychiatric manifestations. Despite growing evidence of shared pathophysiological mechanisms, neither condition has established diagnostic biomarkers or disease-modifying treatments. Cognitive behavior therapy (CBT), when appropriately implemented, may serve as one component of comprehensive care. This review examines the neuropsychiatric manifestations, management principles, and implementation considerations for CBT in long COVID, drawing insights from ME/CFS experience.

sec.title: Main body
sec.p: Our analyses revealed substantial overlap between patients with long COVID and ME/CFS including immune dysregulation, neuroinflammation, and metabolic dysfunction while identifying distinct features in disease trajectories. Evidence suggests ME/CFS may represent a severe phenotype in a subset of patients with long COVID. Management principles applicable to both conditions include patient validation, comprehensive needs assessment, individualized energy management, symptom-specific interventions, and comorbidity management. Current clinical trials demonstrate methodological evolution in CBT implementation, from traditional protocols to digital platforms. Moderate-certainty evidence indicates CBT may reduce fatigue and improve cognitive function in long COVID. However, substantial heterogeneity exists in both intervention characteristics and condition definitions. Implementation success requires provider competency, terminological precision, and individualized approaches that respect energy limitations. Careful monitoring for post-exertional symptom exacerbation is essential. We emphasize that these approaches do not imply these conditions are primarily psychological.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Our review synthesizes current evidence on CBT in long COVID management, considering lessons from ME/CFS. Substantial challenges remain in standardizing terminology, strengthening trial methodology, and determining optimal implementation strategies. Future research should incorporate objective outcome measures alongside subjective reports, while clinical practice should consider how cognitive-behavioral approaches might contribute to comprehensive care plans tailored to individual patient needs. This approach recognizes that addressing both physical and psychological dimensions of these conditions may enhance treatment outcomes, while acknowledging the individualized nature of patient responses to different therapeutic elements.

institution-wrap.institution: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2025

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The COVID-19 pandemic has left in its wake a lingering health crisis: long COVID. This condition, characterized by persistent symptoms following SARS-CoV-2 infection, has emerged as an important challenge to global public health and economic stability. Recent meta-analyses indicate that 10%–20% of COVID-19 patients develop prolonged symptoms [1] with potentially years-long persistence and substantial economic impact [2, 3]. A comprehensive 3-year follow-up study revealed a significant accumulation of disability-adjusted life years, with neurological and mental disorders emerging as the primary contributors to this long-term health burden [4].

Long COVID, also known as post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC), presents a spectrum of symptoms that strongly overlap with those of myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS). Early recognition of this overlap led to the inclusion of long COVID as one of five pillars in a seminal perspective outlining a comprehensive COVID-19 strategy in the New England Journal of Medicine [5]. This strategic approach acknowledged the symptomatic and pathophysiological similarities of the two conditions, raising critical questions about therapeutic crossover potential.

Despite extensive research, neither long COVID nor ME/CFS has established diagnostic biomarkers, and our pathophysiological understanding of both conditions remains incomplete [6–9]. This limitation necessitates treatment approaches focused primarily on symptom management and functional support rather than targeting specific disease processes, a key consideration for clinical practice.

Given these circumstances, cognitive behavior therapy (CBT) has emerged as one potential intervention for the neuropsychiatric symptoms of long COVID that warrants systematic evaluation. Meta-analyses conducted during the pandemic, though not specifically focused on long COVID populations, demonstrate that CBT and physical exercise interventions are associated with significant improvements in both depressive (standardized mean difference = − 0.72, 95% confidence interval [CI]: −0.93 to − 0.51) and anxiety symptoms (standardized mean difference = − 0.65, 95% CI: −0.85 to − 0.45) [10]. More directly relevant, the ReCOVer trial reported a reduction in severe fatigue (mean difference: −8.5 points on the Chalder Fatigue Scale; 95% CI: −12.3 to − 4.7; p < 0.001) compared with usual care [11]. However, methodological discussions following this trial have highlighted important considerations regarding expectation bias and the need for more robust control conditions [12, 13], echoing earlier debates surrounding behavioral interventions for ME/CFS.

It is important to clarify that considering CBT as a component of management does not imply that either long COVID or ME/CFS are primarily psychological conditions. Rather, CBT offers strategies for addressing symptoms and functional impairments within the context of conditions with clear biological foundations. The positioning of CBT varies across guidelines and evolving evidence. Current National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines for ME/CFS position CBT as an approach to help manage symptoms and reduce illness-associated distress [14], while noting these recommendations should not be automatically applied to long COVID. It should be noted that illness-associated distress has been regarded as both a contributing and perpetuating factor in CBT studies to date, including in the ReCOVer trial [11]. Meanwhile, recent meta-analyses suggest potentially broader therapeutic effects for both conditions [15, 16].
The present review addresses three fundamental questions:

list-item.p: To what extent do neuropsychiatric manifestations in long COVID parallel those in ME/CFS?
What are the key principles in managing ME/CFS that can inform long COVID care, and how might CBT be appropriately adapted within these principles?
What implementation considerations are essential for optimizing therapeutic approaches that address the multifaceted nature of these conditions?

sec.p: Through a systematic analysis of current evidence, ongoing clinical trials, and emerging treatment paradigms, we have evaluated the potential for cross-condition therapeutic applications while acknowledging the distinct features of each condition. Our aim is to provide clinicians with a balanced perspective that integrates historical insights from ME/CFS with emerging evidence for long COVID.

sec.title: Long covid and ME/CFS: shared and distinct features
Symptomatology and clinical presentation
sec.p: Recent meta-analyses have identified fatigue as the predominant symptom in long COVID (28% of patients), closely followed by cognitive impairments (27.6%) [17]. Similarly, ME/CFS is defined by severe fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cognitive impairments, and sleep disturbances [6–8]. Pain manifestations represent another important overlap, with both conditions reporting substantial rates of joint and muscle pain and headaches [17]. The symptom overlap further extends to psychiatric manifestations [2, 18], with reported rates of depression (22.44%), anxiety (27.77%), and sleep disturbances (19.13%) among individuals with long COVID [17]. This pattern mirrors historical observations in other post-viral syndromes; one study reported that 40% of SARS survivors experienced active psychiatric illness and chronic fatigue at follow-up, with 27% meeting the Fukuda criteria at 4 years [3]. Similar patterns have emerged in recent long COVID data; a 3-year follow-up study demonstrated substantial neurological and mental health sequelae, with cumulative rates of 79.5 and 56.9 per 1,000 individuals, respectively [4].

Autonomic dysfunction represents another important shared feature between the conditions. Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome occurs in 27%–50% of ME/CFS cases [19, 20] and affects 15%–33% of those experiencing long COVID [21]. These rates suggest a common underlying disturbance in autonomic regulation, potentially contributing to the broader symptom complexes observed in both conditions. The manifestation of postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome in these conditions often correlates with the severity of fatigue and cognitive symptoms, suggesting potential mechanistic links between autonomic dysfunction and core clinical features.

Despite these similarities, important distinctions exist in the broader symptom profiles of the two conditions. Individuals with long COVID frequently report symptoms that are uncommon in ME/CFS, such as a loss of smell and taste, chronic cough, and persistent thirst [6, 22]. The persistence of respiratory symptoms may reflect the specific pathogenic mechanisms of SARS-CoV-2 infection. Conversely, ME/CFS patients more frequently experience painful lymph nodes and heightened chemical sensitivity [6]. These distinctions in symptom profiles suggest both shared and condition-specific pathophysiological mechanisms.

sec.title: Pathophysiological mechanisms
sec.p: The underlying pathophysiological mechanisms also demonstrate substantial overlap between the conditions, and are characterized by complex interactions between the immune, metabolic, and neurological systems [6–9]. We emphasize that while evidence supports these mechanisms, our understanding remains evolving and incomplete, with many proposed pathways representing hypotheses under active investigation rather than definitively established processes.

Immune dysregulation is evident in both conditions, characterized by T cell exhaustion, reduced natural killer cell function, and altered inflammatory markers [9]. These immune alterations may contribute to the persistent inflammatory state observed in both conditions, which potentially drives many of the shared symptoms.

Metabolic dysfunction manifests through impaired mitochondrial function and altered adenosine triphosphate production, potentially explaining the shared symptoms of fatigue and exercise intolerance [7, 9]. Specific alterations in energy metabolism include disrupted electron transport chain function and abnormal fatty acid oxidation. These metabolic disturbances may underlie the characteristic post-exertional malaise observed in both conditions, although the precise mechanisms of energy impairment may differ.

Neuroinflammatory processes represent another shared pathophysiological feature. Neuroimaging evidence has identified widespread microglial activation among individuals with long COVID, particularly in vascularized regions including the midcingulate cortex, basal ganglia, and thalamus, correlating with markers of vascular dysfunction [23]. Complementing these findings, elevated plasma neurofilament light chain levels in patients with post-COVID cognitive symptoms suggest ongoing neuronal injury [24]. This neuroinflammation likely results from multiple mechanisms including direct cerebromicrovascular damage, as SARS-CoV-2 can infect endothelial cells via ACE2 receptors, triggering blood-brain barrier disruption, impaired neurovascular coupling, and compromised cerebral blood flow [25].

Viral persistence, particularly of reactivated herpesviruses, is associated with ongoing neuroimmune dysfunction in both conditions, and may contribute to cognitive symptoms and autonomic disturbances [6, 8, 9]. Additionally, autoantibodies against central and autonomic nervous system targets suggest a potential autoimmune component in both conditions [6].

sec.title: Diagnostic considerations
sec.p: The symptomatic similarities between long COVID and ME/CFS present important diagnostic challenges. Studies applying ME/CFS diagnostic criteria to individuals with long COVID have revealed varying degrees of concordance. Although 52.7% of individuals with long COVID meet the Fukuda criteria [26], the application of more stringent criteria, such as the Canadian Consensus Criteria, typically yields different results depending on methodology and self-reporting factors [7, 26, 27].

Regarding etiology, both conditions share notable similarities. ME/CFS is increasingly recognized as an infection-associated chronic illness, with up to 80% of patients developing symptoms following an acute viral-like illness [28]. Its development involves a complex interplay of genetic vulnerability and environmental triggers, including infections, stressors, genetics, and environmental factors [28–30].

The etiology of long COVID remains under active investigation, with current hypotheses including immune dysregulation, possible viral component persistence or reactivation, mitochondrial alterations, and metabolic disruptions [31]. While these mechanisms have been proposed based on observational and preliminary studies, definitive pathophysiological understanding remains incomplete. Recent critical analyses [32] have questioned specific hypotheses like microclot formation, highlighting the evolving nature of our understanding and the need for caution in treatment approaches without established evidence. The heterogeneity of presentations further suggests that long COVID may represent a spectrum of post-infectious processes rather than a single disease entity.

sec.title: Disease trajectory and recovery patterns
sec.p: The greatest distinction between the two conditions lies in their etiologies and disease trajectories. Although long COVID follows a confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, ME/CFS can be triggered by various pathogens or non-infectious causes; up to 41% of patients attribute their condition to severe stress events [7]. Furthermore, although up to 75% of ME/CFS cases are preceded by an infection-like episode, the specific agent often remains unidentified [6, 33].

The progression patterns also differ notably. ME/CFS typically follows a persistent, potentially lifelong course with rare full recovery [34], whereas long COVID symptoms, although potentially persisting for years, show more variability in progression and recovery patterns [3, 4, 18, 26].

In a study of 465 individuals with long COVID evaluated approximately 70 weeks post-infection, 58% fulfilled the ME/CFS Canadian Consensus Criteria [27], with significant discrepancies in self-reporting accuracy. Data from the NIH RECOVER initiative [35] indicates that approximately 4.5% (531 of 11,785) of individuals with prior SARS-CoV-2 infection met the 2015 Institute of Medicine criteria for ME/CFS at least six months after infection, compared to 0.6% (9 of 1,439) among uninfected participants. This represents a 7.5-fold difference in prevalence. Longitudinal time-to-event analysis demonstrated a hazard ratio of 4.93 (95% CI: 3.62–6.71) for developing ME/CFS following SARS-CoV-2 infection. Additionally, 88.7% of individuals who developed post-COVID-19 ME/CFS also met criteria for PASC, indicating overlap between these conditions.

A cohort study following more than 4,500 adults showed COVID-19 patients maintained 4.32 times higher risk for chronic fatigue throughout 18 months of follow-up (1.8/100 person-years), indicating a persistent rather than delayed effect [36]. Similarly, at 7 months post-infection, a multinational survey [37] found that over 70% of patients reported core ME/CFS symptoms, with 67.5% experiencing significant work disability.

These temporal patterns support the hypothesis that ME/CFS may represent a severe phenotype of long COVID in a subset of patients, with important implications for early intervention and targeted treatment approaches.

sec.title: Management Principles for ME/CFS and Long COVID
sec.p: Treatment strategies for both conditions have evolved substantially, informed by growing pathophysiological understanding and clinical experience. A fundamental shift has occurred in ME/CFS management, with graded exercise therapy no longer recommended in recent guidelines due to concerns about potential adverse effects [14, 38]. This change reflects a growing recognition of the biological basis of these conditions and the importance of avoiding post-exertional symptom exacerbation.

Early recognition and intervention in long COVID have drawn from ME/CFS experience. The US ME/CFS Clinical Coalition advocates for early intervention in long COVID cases showing ME/CFS-like symptoms, suggesting that management strategies can begin as early as 6 months post-infection [39]. This suggestion aligns with the NICE guidelines, which recommend treatment initiation for ME/CFS within 3 months of symptom onset in adults [14]; this also corresponds with the temporal definition of long COVID as per the World Health Organization (WHO).

Currently, there are no FDA-approved, disease-specific treatments for ME/CFS, and no established pharmacological interventions for long COVID. Management centers on several core principles derived from multiple guidelines [14, 29, 39–41]. These include patient validation and education, acknowledging the patient’s lived experience to establish trust; comprehensive needs assessment, evaluating functional impairments and providing appropriate support; energy management strategies, respecting individual symptom patterns and thresholds; symptom-specific interventions, addressing core symptoms through targeted approaches; and comorbidity management, treating conditions that may compound symptom burden.

For long COVID, management approaches have evolved independently while drawing on ME/CFS insights. The WHO clinical management guideline for COVID-19 (2022) suggests educational interventions and skills-training on energy conservation techniques for fatigue management, while noting that symptom-titrated physical activity may be considered for those without post-exertional symptom exacerbation [42]. This guideline mentions CBT as a potential therapeutic option with conditional recommendations for specific symptoms including fatigue, mental health symptoms, and sleep disturbances.

Similarly, the NICE guideline on managing the long-term effects of COVID-19 (2024) recommends individually tailored physical activity approaches with careful symptom monitoring [43]. Notably, this guideline defines “rehabilitation” broadly as “a set of interventions designed to optimize functioning, health and wellbeing, and reduce disability in people with health conditions in interaction with their environment,” explicitly including psychological approaches alongside physical interventions.

Additional guidance from the Korean Society of Infectious Diseases (2024) offers more specific recommendations, positioning CBT as a first-line treatment for sleep disorders in long COVID [44]. These varied international approaches reflect the evolving nature of evidence in this field and the recognition that addressing both physical and psychological aspects is essential for comprehensive management.

A critical consideration across all these guidelines and management approaches is the primacy of the “do no harm” principle. Historical experiences with interventions in ME/CFS underscore the potential for harm when strategies are applied without adequate attention to symptom exacerbation, particularly post-exertional malaise. This protective stance, prioritizing patient safety alongside therapeutic efficacy, is especially important when considering physical activity interventions, as discussed in the following section.

sec.title: Physical activity approaches: evidence and controversies
sec.p: Recent evidence regarding physical activity in long COVID reveals significant implementation challenges in clinical practice. One study [45] documented concerning inconsistencies in professional recommendations: healthcare professionals recommended physical activity in 45.7% of cases while discouraging it in 28.3%, with approaches ranging from walking (40%) to graded exercise therapy (25–30%) to pacing and gentle activity (approximately 15% each). Meanwhile, patients reported primarily implementing pacing (78%) rather than graded exercise therapy (22.6%), suggesting important discrepancies between professional recommendations and actual implementation strategies.

Patient responses to physical activity demonstrate considerable heterogeneity: 74.8% reported symptom exacerbation following physical activity, yet 21.0% experienced mixed effects and 28.7% reported no worsening. Within this context, patient-preference approaches have been examined [46] where exercise guidance was provided only to those who specifically requested it (17% of participants). Findings that patients with higher post-exertional malaise scores showed improvements challenge simplistic categorizations but must be interpreted within specific implementation contexts.

This widespread terminological ambiguity and implementation inconsistency underscores the need for precise, standardized definitions and individualized approaches that respect patient symptom responses. Contemporary guidelines for both conditions emphasize individualized energy management over standardized exercise protocols, with particular attention to avoiding symptom exacerbation.

sec.title: Evidence for CBT in Long COVID and ME/CFS
sec.p: CBT represents one of the most extensively studied psychological approaches for long COVID, although multidisciplinary programs and mindfulness-based interventions are also being actively investigated [47]. A living systematic review [48] found moderate-certainty evidence that CBT reduces fatigue (mean difference − 8.4, 95% CI − 13.11 to − 3.69) and improves concentration (mean difference − 5.2, 95% CI − 7.97 to − 2.43) in individuals with long COVID. The authors concluded that CBT probably reduces fatigue and improves cognitive function in patients with long COVID and contributes to recovery and meaningful improvements.

The therapeutic framework of CBT for managing these conditions has evolved significantly. Contemporary approaches integrate cognitive, emotional, behavioral, and symptomatic components through targeted interventions (Fig. 1). This approach addresses several critical areas, including the validation of patient experiences, comprehensive needs assessment, and development of effective pacing strategies [29, 40]. Validation is particularly important because patients often report that their concerns are dismissed or misdiagnosed as purely psychological conditions [29].

caption.p: Integrative Cognitive Behavior Therapy (CBT) Framework for Long COVID Management Conceptual model illustrating the interconnected components of CBT intervention in long COVID. The framework demonstrates bidirectional relationships between the cognitive, emotional, behavioral, and symptomatic domains. Additionally, specific therapeutic targets are indicated for each component. Arrows represent the dynamic interactions between domains. Key psychological factors that may be amenable to CBT intervention are highlighted, thus emphasizing the therapeutic potential of the model for addressing the complex manifestations of long COVID Note: “Behavioral activation” in this context refers to engagement in meaningful activities that may provide pleasure or a sense of accomplishment, and is not a recommendation to exercise. When applying this CBT principle to Long COVID management, careful energy management should be considered, incorporating pacing strategies and selection of activities that are appropriate and manageable for each individual

sec.p: Recent meta-analyses of CBT for ME/CFS have provided important insights. One systematic review [49] found that CBT was more effective than controls in reducing fatigue (g = −0.52, 95% CI −0.69 to −0.35), improving perceived health, and alleviating depression and anxiety, with effects maintained for fatigue and anxiety at long-term follow-up. Additionally, an individual patient data meta-analysis [50] demonstrated beneficial effects of CBT on fatigue severity (β = −11.46, 95% CI − 15.13 to − 7.79, p < 0.001), functional impairment, and physical functioning in ME/CFS patients. This analysis found no indication that patients meeting different case definitions or reporting additional symptoms like post-exertional malaise benefited less from CBT.

While these findings challenge assumptions about differential response patterns, it is important to acknowledge that many earlier studies had limitations in how post-exertional malaise was defined, measured, and reported as an outcome. Few studies accurately validated post-exertional malaise as a distinct phenomenon or tracked its changes systematically across interventions. Similarly, variations in how dropout rates were calculated and reported across studies may affect interpretation of treatment acceptability and potential adverse effects. The meta-analysis [49] addressed this by examining different dimensions of study attrition, providing a more comprehensive picture of patient engagement with these interventions.

sec.title: Implementation Considerations for CBT
sec.p: These findings must be contextualized within an evolving evidence landscape. While NICE guidelines [14] position CBT as a supportive approach for ME/CFS, explicitly noting it is “not a treatment or cure,” recent meta-analyses challenge this limited framing. The individual patient data meta-analysis [50] and systematic review [49] suggest CBT may offer meaningful therapeutic benefits for many patients across symptom domains, with effects maintained at long-term follow-up. These findings support a more nuanced understanding of the potential role of CBT in management strategies while acknowledging the need for appropriate implementation within a comprehensive care model.

The effectiveness of CBT depends strongly on provider competency and appropriate implementation. Qualitative evidence indicates that ME/CFS patients find CBT beneficial when delivered by therapists who understand the complexities of the condition. By contrast, inappropriate delivery may cause harm, which has led to specific recommendations regarding provider qualifications and clinical supervision requirements [14]. Through psychoeducation, the development of adaptive coping strategies, and techniques for managing sleep disturbances and cognitive difficulties, CBT provides a structured framework for addressing both the physical and psychological aspects of long COVID and ME/CFS.

The practice of CBT in these conditions requires the careful consideration of several factors. Provider competency remains paramount; therapists need a thorough understanding of the complexity of the condition as well as regular clinical supervision [14, 40]. Moreover, treatment plans must be individualized and respect patient energy limits. The regular monitoring and adjustment of interventions may ensure optimal outcomes and prevent potential harm through careful attention to post-exertional symptom exacerbation. This systematic approach, illustrated in Fig. 1, allows for targeted interventions that acknowledge the cyclical nature of symptoms while supporting comprehensive patient care.

Beyond these specific conditions, CBT has demonstrated efficacy across numerous medical conditions beyond traditional psychiatric applications, including sleep disorders [51], chronic pain [52], functional gastrointestinal conditions [53], diabetes management [54], and cardiovascular health [55]. This broader evidence reinforces the potential value of CBT in addressing specific symptoms common in long COVID and ME/CFS within a comprehensive approach that acknowledges the physiological nature of these disorders.

sec.title: Clinical evidence for CBT in long COVID management
Current evidence base
sec.p: Interventions targeting mental health, cognition, and psychological well-being in individuals with long COVID have previously been systematically evaluated [56]. However, this early systematic review, while noting the potential utility of CBT, emphasized two critical needs: rigorous trial design, and appropriate patient stratification reflecting the heterogeneous nature of long COVID presentations. Moreover, their analysis was limited to trials registered by May 31, 2022, when completed trials were relatively scarce.

To address this evidence gap, we conducted an updated systematic search of ClinicalTrials.gov using the terms “(Post-Acute COVID19 Syndrome OR PASC OR Post Acute Sequelae of COVID 19 OR Long COVID) AND (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy OR Cognitive Therapy OR Cognitive Behavior Therapy OR Cognitive Behaviour Therapy OR CBT).” This identified 189 potentially relevant trials, with 10 meeting final inclusion criteria of examining CBT or CBT-based interventions as primary or major treatment components for adults with long COVID. The characteristics of these trials are summarized in Table 1, revealing marked evolution in study designs and implementation approaches since the pandemic onset.

caption.p: Characteristics of clinical trials that evaluate cognitive behavior therapy for long COVID

tr.td: Long COVID (symptoms persisting more than 3 months after PCR/antigen-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection), age 21–65 years, moderate cognitive impairment (MOCA score 18 or below)
Digital CBT with health coach vs. medication; 12 weeks

td.p: Primary: Cognitive function (MOCA, BrainCheck, ImPACT)
Secondary: Depression, quality of life, disability

tr.td: Long COVID (symptoms persisting more than 2 months within 3 months of onset), age 18–45 years, dyspnea grade 2 or above on mMRC scale, non-hospitalized
Three arms: PLB + CBT + kinesio tape vs. PLB + CBT + breathing exercise vs. PLB + CBT; 6 weeks

td.p: Primary: Dyspnea (mMRC)
Secondary: Oxygen saturation, fatigue, physical performance, quality of life

tr.td: Long COVID (PCR-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection with symptoms persisting more than 3 months causing functional disability), age 16 years or older

td.p: Primary: Physical functioning (SF-36)
Secondary: Fatigue, depression/anxiety, recovery, adverse effects

tr.td: Long COVID (fatigue symptoms persisting more than 3 months after COVID-19 infection with CFQ-11 score 16 or above), age 18 years or older, German-speaking
Digital CBT + TAU vs. TAU; 3 months

td.p: Primary: Fatigue (CFQ-11)
Secondary: Depression, quality of life, functioning, somatic symptoms, anxiety

tr.td: Long COVID and select neurological conditions with cognitive impairment (1.0 SD or more below age/education norms in at least two cognitive domains), age 21–65 years

td.p: Primary: Feasibility outcomes
Secondary: Community integration, quality of life, cognitive function

tr.td: Long COVID (PCR/antigen-confirmed within 6 months), age 18–40 years, presenting with insomnia, anxiety, and depression
CBT-based behavioral intervention: Face-to-face vs. teleconsultation; 4 weeks

td.p: Primary: Sleep quality
Secondary: Depression, anxiety, quality of life

tr.td: Long COVID with psychological symptoms (chronic pain, anxiety, depression, and/or insomnia requiring medication) in ICU survivors who had COVID-19.

td.p: Primary: Chronic pain, anxiety, depression, insomnia
Secondary: Not specified

tr.td: Long COVID as per NHS/NICE criteria (symptoms more than 12 weeks post-infection), age 18 years or older, no previous COVID-19 hospitalization

td.p: Primary: SF-36
Secondary: Quality of life, fatigue, sleep, physical function, cognitive function, anxiety, depression

tr.td: Long COVID (PCR-confirmed with dyspnea and/or fatigue persisting more than 3 months), age 40–80 years, increased mMRC score compared with pre-infection
Multimodal intervention (12 sessions each of CBT and physical, nutritional, occupational, and speech therapy) vs. TAU

td.p: Primary: Cardiopulmonary exercise capacity
Secondary: Fatigue, mobility, exercise capacity, anxiety, depression, cognitive function, independence, quality of life

tr.td: Long COVID meeting Oxford CFS criteria (fatigue as a principal symptom present > 50% of the time over 6 months after PCR-confirmed infection), age 18–34 years

td.p: Primary: Fatigue severity, aerobic capacity, grip strength, fall risk
Secondary: Not specified

table-wrap-foot.p: Data compiled from a systematic search of ClinicalTrials.gov for trials registered between May 2022 and October 2024, with completed trial results reported during this period. Trials were included if they examined CBT or CBT-based interventions as primary or major treatment components for adults with long COVID. Studies are categorized by status: (1) Completed or Early Terminated, and (2) Other Registered. Delivery modes included in-person (individual, group, outpatient, and multimodal) and digital (mobile app, web-based, and teleconsultation) approaches. Sample sizes are presented as target/actual enrollment; a dash (-) indicates that actual enrollment has not yet been reported.

Abbreviations: ACT Acceptance and commitment therapy, CBT Cognitive behavioral therapy, CFQ−11 Chalder Fatigue Scale, CFS Chronic fatigue syndrome, CRT Cognitive rehabilitation therapy, GET Graded exercise therapy; ImPACT, Immediate Post-Concussion Assessment and Cognitive Testing, MBSR Mindfulness-based stress reduction, mMRC Modified Medical Research Council Scale, MoCA Montreal Cognitive Assessment, NHS/NICE National Health Service/National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, PCR Polymerase chain reaction, PLB Pursed lip breathing, RCT Randomized controlled trial, SD Standard deviation, SF-36 36-Item Short Form Health Survey, TAU Treatment as usual.

sec.p: The review of these clinical trials revealed substantial diversity in both methodological and interventional characteristics. Digital platforms have emerged as an important innovation, with trials incorporating mobile applications supported by health coaching [59] or web-based interventions integrated with usual care [60]. These technological adaptations reflect both pandemic-driven necessities and therapeutic innovations, potentially increasing treatment accessibility while maintaining intervention fidelity.

Patient selection and classification have become increasingly refined over time, with trials now targeting specific symptom clusters and functional disabilities. For example, one trial specifically addresses cognitive impairment using combined cognitive rehabilitation therapy and CBT [61], whereas another focus on functional disability as a primary outcome of large-scale pragmatic trials [62]. The definition and operationalization of CBT itself varies considerably across studies, ranging from traditional protocols that are focused on cognitive restructuring and behavioral activation to third-wave approaches incorporating mindfulness-based interventions [63] or Acceptance and Commitment Therapy [64].

sec.title: Methodological considerations in key trials
sec.p: Beyond the general methodological challenges affecting this field, specific key trials warrant targeted examination. The ReCOVer trial [11], while demonstrating statistically significant reductions in self-reported fatigue, presents specific methodological considerations that influence interpretation of its findings. Most notably, the trial’s reliance on subjective self-report measures without corresponding objective outcomes creates vulnerability to response bias in a non-blinded intervention context. The absence of objective measures that were specified in the original protocol (such as actometer results) leaves important questions about physical function improvements unaddressed [12].

The trial’s design also lacked active control conditions with equivalent expectation-setting and therapeutic contact time, creating difficulty in distinguishing specific intervention effects from non-specific factors such as increased attention and support. These design elements parallel methodological concerns previously raised regarding the PACE trial for ME/CFS [65], where post-hoc outcome definition changes, lowered thresholds for recovery, and discrepancies between subjective improvements and objective measures generated substantial controversy in the field [66, 67].

It is worth noting that current clinical guidelines have shifted away from recommending graded exercise therapy for patients with post-exertional malaise, a core symptom in many ME/CFS and long COVID patients. While a recent Cochrane review [68] maintained earlier conclusions supporting exercise therapy for chronic fatigue syndrome, it is important to recognize that despite its 2024 publication date, its evidence base remains unchanged from earlier versions, with literature searches ending in 2014. Five of the eight included studies employed the Oxford criteria, which do not require post-exertional malaise as a diagnostic criterion, potentially introducing significant patient population heterogeneity. This historical context underscores the importance of rigorous methodology, appropriate patient selection, and careful monitoring of adverse events in current and future research.

It is important to acknowledge that psychotherapy research broadly, including CBT trials for long COVID, faces inherent methodological challenges. Most notably, the inability to fully blind participants to their treatment condition creates unavoidable awareness of intervention receipt, potentially influencing expectation effects and subjective outcome reporting. This limitation underscores the importance of incorporating objective outcome measures and carefully designed active control conditions that can help distinguish specific therapeutic effects from non-specific factors related to therapeutic attention and expectation.

Beyond design considerations in existing trials, emerging evidence offers important nuance regarding the mechanisms underlying activity patterns in long COVID. A recent ecological momentary assessment study [69] challenges traditional CBT assumptions about negative affect and activity. The findings demonstrated that perceived negative affect was not significantly associated with subsequent physical activity levels, suggesting that fear-avoidance models may not fully explain activity limitation in long COVID. Interestingly, the same study found positive affect significantly predicted increased physical activity (average increase of 1.53 min of moderate/vigorous activity).

These findings can be interpreted within a more nuanced framework of CBT mechanisms in long COVID. The lack of association between negative affect and activity levels challenges simplified fear-avoidance models, while the significant relationship between positive affect and increased physical activity suggests potential value in therapeutic approaches that enhance positive emotional states, an element often incorporated in contemporary CBT protocols. This evidence underscores the importance of moving beyond dichotomous conceptualizations toward more integrated models of symptom experience and management.

sec.title: Person-centered approaches: emerging evidence
sec.p: Several recent trials exemplify promising person-centered approaches to CBT for long COVID. One study protocol [70] described an interdisciplinary rehabilitation program integrating CBT and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy within a comprehensive care model that validates patients’ complaints while involving multiple specialists, aligning with current guidelines for comprehensive care.

Evidence from key clinical trials supports these approaches. The previously mentioned ReCOVer trial [11] demonstrated significant and sustained fatigue reduction through a personalized 17-week program. Similarly, two other rehabilitation programs showed notable results: a brief outpatient program with cognitive-behavioral elements [46] improved physical function (difference: 9.2; 95% CI, 4.3–14.2; p < 0.001) with stronger benefits among those with higher post-exertional malaise scores, while a concentrated group rehabilitation program [71] achieved high completion rates (99%) with significant improvements in functional levels and health status.

These complementary approaches reflect our evolving understanding of long COVID and highlight the importance of tailoring approaches to individual patient needs and preferences. The high completion rates in some programs suggest that appropriately implemented, personalized approaches may address concerns about acceptability that have been raised in previous discussions of behavioral interventions for post-viral conditions.

sec.title: Terminological and definitional challenges
sec.p: Evaluation of the role of CBT in long COVID management requires careful consideration of how interventions are defined and categorized. The most recent living systematic review to date [48] categorizes CBT as a “behavioural intervention,” distinct from “physical activity and rehabilitation interventions.” This review reported moderate-certainty evidence that CBT is effective in reducing fatigue (mean difference − 8.4, 95% CI − 13.11 to − 3.69) and improving concentration among individuals with long COVID, suggesting meaningful therapeutic effects.

However, intervention terminology varies significantly across studies and guidelines. The NICE guideline on managing the long-term effects of COVID-19 (2024) [43] does not explicitly mention CBT but defines “rehabilitation” broadly as “a set of interventions designed to optimise functioning, health and wellbeing, and reduce disability in people with health conditions in interaction with their environment,” explicitly including psychological elements alongside physical approaches. This inclusive definition contrasts with narrower categorizations found in some systematic reviews.

This terminological inconsistency is further exemplified in clinical trials, where interventions labeled as “rehabilitation” often incorporate cognitive-behavioral elements [46, 71], while others explicitly define their intervention as CBT [11]. These varied classification approaches across international guidelines and research protocols complicate direct comparisons of evidence but highlight the emerging recognition that addressing both physical and psychological aspects of long COVID is important for meaningful recovery.

sec.title: Future directions and research priorities
sec.p: Analysis of ongoing and planned trials suggests several critical directions for advancing the field. The integration of digital delivery platforms shows promise for increasing therapeutic accessibility, although questions remain regarding therapeutic alliance and engagement. Several trials [46, 70, 72, 73] combine CBT with other interventions within multidisciplinary approaches, reflecting the complex nature of long COVID symptoms and the need for comprehensive treatment strategies.

Future research must prioritize addressing current methodological limitations. This includes designing active control conditions that match therapeutic contact time and expectation effects to distinguish specific intervention mechanisms from non-specific factors. The controversies surrounding previous trials highlight the importance of transparent reporting practices, including adherence to pre-registered outcome measures without post-hoc modifications. Additionally, trials should systematically track and report adverse events, particularly incidents of post-exertional symptom exacerbation that have been central to debates in this field.

Equally critical is establishing clear operational definitions and standardized terminology across intervention studies. Terms like ‘graded exercise,’ ‘pacing,’ and ‘gentle activity’ are interpreted with significant variability by both patients and providers [45]. This terminological ambiguity creates significant barriers to effective clinical implementation and accurate research interpretation, as neither healthcare providers nor patients can consistently understand what specific interventions entail.

To address these challenges, the field requires standardized CBT protocols specifically tailored for long COVID, yet flexible enough to address varied presentations. Key research priorities include integrating biological markers alongside patient-reported outcomes to establish objective correlates of improvement; extending follow-up periods beyond the typical 3–6 months to assess long-term durability of effects; evaluating cost-effectiveness across delivery modalities, including digital interventions; and determining optimal intervention timing for different patient subgroups are also essential.

Methodologically, implementing standardized measurement of post-exertional malaise using validated instruments, establishing comprehensive adverse event monitoring with standardized definitions, and ensuring consistent reporting of attrition patterns, including specific reasons for dropout, will strengthen future research. Finally, examining how CBT components can be adapted to address post-exertional malaise and energy management represents a promising direction for enhancing therapeutic efficacy while respecting the biological foundations of these conditions.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: The fundamental challenge in evaluating CBT for long COVID lies in the heterogeneity of both condition definitions and intervention characteristics. The boundaries between long COVID and ME/CFS remain imprecise, with varying diagnostic criteria and overlapping symptom profiles complicating patient classification. Similarly, interventions labeled as “CBT” maintain some consistency, while many rehabilitation and multicomponent interventions incorporate varying degrees of cognitive-behavioral elements without clear delineation of their extent or implementation fidelity. This dual ambiguity makes it difficult to determine which components are most effective for specific symptom clusters across heterogeneous patient populations.

Methodologically, although this review incorporated relevant guidelines, meta-analyses, and key studies in this field, this report represents a narrative synthesis rather than a formal systematic review. The focused search of ClinicalTrials.gov for trial identification may have missed relevant international studies not registered there, particularly those using alternative terminology for similar interventions. Additionally, most studies have relatively short follow-up periods, limiting assessment of long-term efficacy in conditions that may persist for years. The rapidly evolving understanding of long COVID means that condition definitions and outcome measures continue to develop, complicating cross-study comparisons.

Implementation challenges include provider competency requirements and integration within existing healthcare systems. Clear communication about CBT’s therapeutic potential and its appropriate positioning within comprehensive care is essential to avoid misrepresenting long COVID as primarily psychological and to maintain patient trust. This requires acknowledging both documented benefits and the importance of implementing CBT in a manner that respects the biological foundations of these conditions.

Despite these limitations, appropriately implemented CBT delivered by knowledgeable providers shows promise for many patients as part of a multifaceted management approach. Future research with standardized terminology, longer follow-up periods, and rigorous methodology including objective outcome measures will help clarify the role of cognitive-behavioral approaches in long COVID care.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Our narrative review has addressed three fundamental questions regarding the role of CBT in long COVID management by drawing insights from experiences with ME/CFS.

First, we identified substantial overlaps between long COVID and ME/CFS in their neuropsychiatric symptom profiles and underlying pathophysiological mechanisms, including immune dysregulation, metabolic alterations, and neuroinflammatory processes. While these similarities suggest a significant shared basis for symptom management, critical distinctions in symptom patterns and disease trajectories, such as the greater variability in recovery patterns and specific respiratory sequelae in long COVID, must also guide clinical practice and research approaches. The evidence indicates that a subset of long COVID patients develops presentations that fulfill ME/CFS diagnostic criteria, suggesting that ME/CFS may represent a severe phenotype of long COVID in some patients.

Second, ME/CFS management principles provide a valuable framework for long COVID care, emphasizing patient validation, comprehensive functional assessment, tailored energy management, symptom-specific interventions, and comorbidity management. Within this framework, the available clinical evidence suggests CBT holds promise as a potentially effective component of a comprehensive care model. Recent systematic reviews and meta-analyses indicate CBT may contribute to reductions in fatigue, enhanced cognitive function, and improved functional status in patients with long COVID and ME/CFS. However, methodological limitations in existing studies, particularly regarding standardized measurement of post-exertional malaise and comprehensive reporting of treatment acceptability, necessitate interpretive caution. Given the absence of definitive disease-modifying therapies, these findings warrant consideration while acknowledging ongoing debates about their interpretation. Most importantly, these approaches must be contextualized within the understanding that neither long COVID nor ME/CFS are primarily psychological conditions; rather, CBT addresses cognitive, emotional, and behavioral aspects within conditions with established biological foundations.

Third, optimal implementation of therapeutic strategies, including CBT, requires critical considerations. Historical experiences from ME/CFS highlight the imperative to rigorously uphold the “do no harm” principle, with careful attention to adverse events, particularly post-exertional symptom exacerbation. Thus, future research must systematically incorporate objective outcome measures alongside subjective reports and maintain rigorous safety monitoring. Given recent evidence indicating heterogeneous patient responses to various physical activity approaches, individualized and symptom-responsive energy management strategies, such as pacing, warrant careful consideration in clinical practice, despite ongoing debates regarding their optimal application. Clear operational definitions, precise terminological consistency, provider competence, and appropriate therapeutic settings are essential prerequisites to realizing the therapeutic potential of CBT and related interventions.

To advance the field, future trials must address current methodological limitations through standardized measurement approaches, particularly for core symptoms like post-exertional malaise, while integrating biological markers with patient-reported outcomes. In clinical practice, providers should adopt a balanced, evidence-informed approach that positions CBT as one component of multidisciplinary care, emphasizing individualization, rigorous safety monitoring, and continuous adaptation based on evolving evidence. This integrated approach acknowledges the complex interplay between biological mechanisms and psychological factors while respecting both the potential effectiveness and limitations of CBT in long COVID management.

def.p: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We thank Bronwen Gardner, PhD, from Edanz (https://jp.edanz.com/ac) for editing a draft of this manuscript.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: N.T. conceptualized the study, conducted the systematic search of clinical trials, analyzed the data, created visualizations (prepared Table 1; figure 1), and drafted the manuscript. H.K. provided critical review, theoretical insights, and substantive revisions to the manuscript. Both authors read and approved the final manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work was supported by the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science [Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (C) JP23K02953 to N.T.].

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: Data sharing is not applicable to this article as no datasets were generated or analyzed during the current study.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
